questions.question: When a host decides to record a session in Zoom, which two primary storage options are typically available?

answerOptions.text: Local Recording and Cloud Recording
answerOptions.rationale: Zoom allows users to save files directly to their computer's hard drive or to Zoom's online storage servers for easier sharing.

answerOptions.text: Internal Memory and External Flash Drive
answerOptions.rationale: While these involve hardware, Zoom classifies recording locations based on whether the data is stored on the host's device or the service provider's servers.

answerOptions.text: Cloud Storage and Social Media Stream
answerOptions.rationale: Streaming to social media is a separate broadcast function rather than a standard archival recording method.

answerOptions.text: Browser Cache and Email Attachment
answerOptions.rationale: Recordings are usually too large for cache or email systems and require dedicated file paths or cloud hosting.

questions.hint: Consider whether the file stays on your physical device or is uploaded to the internet.

questions.question: What is the primary video file format used by Zoom for saving meeting recordings?

answerOptions.text: MP4
answerOptions.rationale: This format is the industry standard for high-quality compressed video and is compatible with almost all modern media players.

answerOptions.text: MOV
answerOptions.rationale: While common in Apple environments, Zoom utilizes a more universal compressed format to ensure cross-platform compatibility.

answerOptions.text: AVI
answerOptions.rationale: This is an older container format that produces much larger file sizes, making it less efficient for web-based meeting recordings.

answerOptions.text: WMV
answerOptions.rationale: This is a proprietary Windows format, and Zoom uses a standard that works natively on both Windows and macOS.

questions.hint: Think of the most common video file extension used for mobile and web playback.

questions.question: In terms of privacy and transparency, how does Zoom handle the start of a recording for participants?

answerOptions.text: Participants receive an audible or visual notification that recording has begun.
answerOptions.rationale: Zoom includes built-in safeguards to inform users they are being recorded, often requiring them to click a consent button.

answerOptions.text: Only the host is notified to avoid distracting the participants.
answerOptions.rationale: Privacy laws generally require that all parties in a recorded session are made aware of the recording.

answerOptions.text: The recording indicator only appears if a participant asks for it.
answerOptions.rationale: The indicator is a default security feature that cannot be hidden by the host in most standard configurations.

answerOptions.text: Participants are only notified if the recording is shared publicly later.
answerOptions.rationale: Notifications must happen at the moment of capture to ensure real-time consent.

questions.hint: Think about legal requirements regarding consent and awareness during digital captures.

questions.question: What is the functional difference between 'Pausing' and 'Stopping' a Zoom recording?

answerOptions.text: Pausing keeps the recording in a single file, while stopping creates a new file when restarted.
answerOptions.rationale: Pausing acts as a temporary break within one continuous file, whereas stopping concludes that specific recording segment.

answerOptions.text: Pausing saves the file to the cloud, while stopping saves it locally.
answerOptions.rationale: The storage destination is determined by the initial setting, not the method of ending the recording.

answerOptions.text: Stopping allows for better video quality than pausing.
answerOptions.rationale: Video quality is determined by bandwidth and settings, not by how the host manages the recording controls.

answerOptions.text: There is no functional difference; both actions result in separate files.
answerOptions.rationale: These controls are distinct to allow hosts to edit out 'dead air' or private breaks without splitting the final output.

questions.hint: Consider how you would want a long meeting to look if you needed to take a five-minute break in the middle.

questions.question: Which of the following is a limitation of 'Local Recording' compared to 'Cloud Recording'?

answerOptions.text: Local Recording is generally not supported on iOS or Android mobile devices.
answerOptions.rationale: Due to storage and file system restrictions on mobile operating systems, Zoom typically requires Cloud Recording for these devices.

answerOptions.text: Local Recording requires a paid subscription, whereas Cloud Recording is free for everyone.
answerOptions.rationale: Actually, Local Recording is often the free option, while Cloud Recording usually requires a licensed account.

answerOptions.text: Local Recording cannot capture audio, only video.
answerOptions.rationale: Local recordings capture the full multimedia experience of the meeting, including audio and shared screens.

answerOptions.text: Local Recording takes significantly longer to process than Cloud Recording.
answerOptions.rationale: Processing time depends on the hardware of the computer, but it is often faster because it does not rely on server queues.

questions.hint: Think about the hardware limitations of a smartphone versus a desktop computer.
